title: Post-and-screw album binding
subtitle: Hard covers on a fully removable page stack
excerpt: Post-and-screw binding uses threaded metal posts that pass through the covers and page stack, with a screw cap on the front face to hold everything together. Pages are fully removable, making it the standard structure for portfolios, photo albums, and sample books where the contents change over time.
sourceNotes: Post-and-screw binding is a modern commercial binding structure with no canonical pre-1928 public-domain source. Cover-board construction and cover-cloth geometry drawn from general bookbinding principles as documented in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), Internet Archive. Structural description synthesised from standard craft-bookbinding practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding post consisting of a threaded barrel (the post) and a matching flat-headed cap (the screw). The post passes through the stack of pages and covers from the back; the screw tightens down onto the front cover face. Also called Chicago screws or sex bolts.
The margin of cover material that wraps around the board edge and is glued to the inside face. A standard turn-in is 15–20 mm wide. Mitre the corners before folding so they lie flat.

The direction in which most fibres in a sheet of paper or board run. Board cut with grain parallel to the spine is more stable than board cut against the grain: it flexes more easily and is less likely to warp when glue is applied.

content.text: Post-and-screw binding, sometimes called Chicago-screw or sex-bolt binding, holds a stack of loose pages between hard covers using threaded metal
post-and-screw

fittings. The spine posts pass through holes drilled or pierced in the cover boards and page stack. The screw cap sits flush on the front cover face. Because the pages are entirely loose, the album can be added to, rearranged, or partially removed at any time by unscrewing the caps. This makes it the standard structure for portfolios, photo albums, and sample books. No sewing or adhesive touches the pages themselves.

items.name: Bookbinding board, 3 mm (2 pieces for covers)
Book cloth for covering
items.qty: enough to cover both boards with a 20 mm turn-in on all four edges

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm for page stock
items.substitutions: Any paper 160–250 gsm; thinner paper is floppy on its own at album scale

items.name: Post-and-screw sets in two lengths
items.qty: 3 sets at 19 mm (for the page body) and 3 sets at 25 mm (extension if pages are thick)
items.substitutions: Aluminium posts are lightest; brass are more durable for a heavy album

items.name: Corner rounder punch (optional)
items.substitutions: Sharp scissors and a coin as a guide also round a corner adequately

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones: blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, never in front of it. Cut away from the body. Keep the hand holding the ruler at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.

Bookbinding adhesives: PVA (polyvinyl acetate) is safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Use a dedicated paste brush and clean it with warm water immediately after use. Never burn book glues to remove them; heat releases acrid fumes.

content.text: Cutting the covers
Cut two boards from the 3 mm bookbinding board. For an A4 album, the front and back boards should be 210 mm wide × 297 mm tall. Check the
grain direction

before cutting: the grain must run parallel to the spine edge (the punched edge). Bend the board lightly in both directions: it bends more easily along the grain. Mark the grain direction on the back face before cutting to avoid confusion.

Cut with a craft knife against the metal ruler in two or three passes. 3 mm board needs three firm passes to cut cleanly; forcing the knife through in one pass risks a skewed cut. Clean the cut edge with a fine pass of the knife along any raised fibres.
Covering the boards

Cut the book cloth 40 mm larger on all four sides than the board (40 mm = 20 mm turn-in each side). Lay the cloth face-down on a clean surface. Apply a thin, even coat of PVA to the board with the paste brush. Place the board glue-side-down onto the cloth, centred with 20 mm borders all around.

Flip the assembly over and rub the cloth down with the bone folder, working from the centre outward to push out air. Mitre the corners: cut at 45° across the corner of the cloth, leaving a small triangle of cloth at the very tip of the board corner (about 1.5 × the board thickness). Fold the head and tail
turn-ins

first, folding the cloth firmly over the board edge and rubbing down with the bone folder. Then fold the spine and foredge turn-ins, tucking the corner cloth neatly. Rub all edges firmly. Cover the second board in the same way.
Press both covered boards under a flat weight for at least an hour to prevent warping. A board that dries under no pressure often cups toward the cloth side.
Preparing the page stock

Cut the 220 gsm cartridge paper to 207 mm × 294 mm, which is 3 mm smaller on all sides than the cover boards, so the board face overhangs the page stack slightly when assembled. This is the standard page-flush cover convention for albums.

Stack the pages and align the spine edge. Clamp with bulldog clips or hold firmly. If using a corner rounder punch, punch all four corners now while the stack is aligned. This is easier than rounding individual pages.
Marking and piercing the post holes

Mark three post positions on the spine edge of the front cover board. For an A4 album, place the top post 30 mm from the head, the bottom post 30 mm from the tail, and the middle post centred between them. All holes sit 12 mm from the spine edge.

Pierce the board with the awl at each mark, drilling straight through the board thickness. Transfer the hole positions to the back board by stacking the boards together and piercing through. Then pierce through the whole page stack. The awl tip should emerge cleanly on the inside face of each sheet.
Assembling

Feed the barrel of each post through the back cover, through the page stack, and through the front cover. Thread the screw cap onto the post from the front. Tighten by hand until the cap sits flush with the cloth surface. Do not use a tool that might strip the thread or crush the board. The cap should be firm but not over-tightened. A page stack that feels stiff to turn indicates the posts are too tight; back off each cap by a half-turn.

To add more pages later, unscrew the caps, remove the posts, insert additional pages, and replace with longer posts if the new stack height requires them. The standard post lengths available are 10 mm, 19 mm, and 25 mm, so plan the initial stack to leave room for additions.
Finishing

Wipe the post caps with a clean cloth to remove any fingerprints. If the book cloth has any adhesive traces on the outer face, wipe clean with a barely damp cloth and allow to dry flat. Store the album upright on a shelf between two neighbour books that support it; laying flat for long periods can warp the covers if the page weight is uneven.
Care

Post-and-screw fittings in aluminium are lightly prone to corrosion if stored in damp conditions. Brass fittings last longer. For an archival-grade album holding photographs, use acid-free pages and a neutral-pH book cloth. Pages that carry mounted photographs add weight: check that the post length is sufficient and that the cap threads are not stripped before adding a heavy addition.
Variations

A two-post binding with posts at head and tail only is the minimal version: faster to make and easier to flip pages, but the centre of the stack bows slightly when a heavy album is held open. Three posts are more stable. For a very large or heavy album (A3 landscape), four posts spread the load better.

The same structure works as a presentation folder: omit the pages entirely, keep both covers, and add a few card dividers between the posts. The result is a flat-fronted portfolio sleeve rather than a bound album.
